questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of severe headache over the left temple, scalp tenderness when brushing her hair, and pain in her jaw when chewing. She feels generally unwell. ESR is 85 mm/hr, CRP 120 mg/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers in an elderly patient is classic for giant cell arteritis. Tension headache does not cause jaw claudication or such high inflammatory markers.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man is admitted with a 4-week history of fever, night sweats, weight loss, and generalised myalgia. He has no headache or visual symptoms. Examination is unremarkable. Investigations: Hb 100 g/L, WCC 12 x10^9/L, platelets 600 x10^9/L, ESR 110 mm/hr, CRP 150 mg/L. Blood cultures negative. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In patients over 70 with fever of unknown origin and very high inflammatory markers, GCA should be considered even without typical cranial symptoms. PMR typically presents with proximal myalgia and stiffness, but not fever and weight loss to this degree. Negative workup for infection and malignancy raises suspicion for large vessel vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with sudden, painless loss of vision in his right eye that occurred 2 hours ago. He also reports occasional headaches and muscle stiffness over the past month. On examination, there is a relative afferent pupillary defect and optic disc swelling in the right eye. ESR is 90 mm/hr. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (due to GCA)
questions.explanation: The combination of sudden vision loss, high ESR, and systemic symptoms (headache, stiffness) suggests arteritic AION due to GCA. Non-arteritic AION typically has lower ESR and no systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman complains of severe pain and stiffness in her shoulders and hips for 2 months, worse in the morning, with difficulty getting out of bed. She also has had a bitemporal headache and tenderness over her scalp. Examination: limited active shoulder abduction due to pain. ESR 60 mm/hr. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Giant cell arteritis with polymyalgia rheumatica
questions.explanation: The typical PMR symptoms (proximal morning stiffness) combined with cranial symptoms (headache, scalp tenderness) indicate GCA. PMR alone does not cause cranial symptoms; fibromyalgia has more widespread pain and normal ESR.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of claudication in her arms, with easy fatigue when using her arms. She has no leg symptoms. She also notes a low-grade fever and weight loss. On examination, blood pressure is 140/80 mmHg in both arms but radial pulses are weak. There is a bruit over the left subclavian artery. ESR 90 mm/hr. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Age >70 with arm claudication, high ESR, and systemic symptoms favours GCA large vessel involvement. Takayasu arteritis typically occurs in younger patients (<40). Atherosclerosis might cause claudication but not such high inflammatory markers.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of bitemporal headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. ESR is 85 mm/hr and CRP is 120 mg/L. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard for confirming giant cell arteritis, especially with classic symptoms and markedly raised inflammatory markers. Ultrasound is an alternative but less specific.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with weight loss, night sweats, and anaemia. ESR is 110 mm/hr. He has no headache or scalp tenderness. PET-CT shows increased FDG uptake in the aorta and subclavian arteries. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Even with atypical large-vessel vasculitis on PET-CT, temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard to confirm giant cell arteritis, as biopsy may show granulomatous inflammation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with a 2-week history of headache, jaw claudication, and an ESR of 90 mm/hr underwent a left temporal artery biopsy. The pathology report shows no evidence of giant cell arteritis. What is the correct interpretation of this result?
options: Requires a repeat biopsy on the same side
Does not exclude giant cell arteritis
questions.explanation: A negative temporal artery biopsy does not exclude giant cell arteritis due to skip lesions. If clinical suspicion is high, treatment should be started; a contralateral biopsy can be considered.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a new-onset headache and an ESR of 78 mm/hr. He has no visual symptoms. You suspect giant cell arteritis and plan to start high-dose steroids. Within what time frame should temporal artery biopsy ideally be performed to maximize diagnostic yield?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy should ideally be performed within 1 week of starting steroids to maintain histopathological sensitivity. Delays reduce yield.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 1-week history of headache and scalp tenderness. ESR is 25 mm/hr and CRP is 8 mg/L. Clinical suspicion for giant cell arteritis remains high due to typical symptoms and age. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Repeat ESR and CRP in 1 week
questions.explanation: Giant cell arteritis can present with normal ESR/CRP in up to 20% of cases. Temporal artery biopsy is the definitive test and should not be delayed by normal inflammatory markers if clinical suspicion is high.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. ESR is 85 mm/hr, CRP 120 mg/L. Temporal artery biopsy is planned. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For newly diagnosed GCA without visual loss, high-dose oral prednisolone (40-60 mg daily) is the first-line treatment. Lower doses (15 mg) are for PMR alone; IV methylprednisolone is reserved for acute visual loss; methotrexate and tocilizumab are second-line steroid-sparing agents.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with known GCA on prednisolone 50 mg daily for 3 days (started after biopsy) develops acute loss of vision in his right eye. Fundoscopy shows a pale optic disc. What is the most appropriate next treatment?
options: Start intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days
Increase oral prednisolone to 80 mg daily
questions.explanation: Acute visual loss in GCA is a medical emergency requiring immediate high-dose IV methylprednisolone to prevent contralateral involvement. Oral steroids alone may be insufficient, and other agents are not first-line in this setting.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with GCA presents with bilateral upper limb claudication and blood pressure discrepancy. PET-CT shows aortitis. She has no visual symptoms. What is the most appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: Large vessel (aortic) involvement in GCA is treated with the same high-dose oral prednisolone as standard GCA. IV pulse therapy is reserved for acute visual loss, not aortitis; surgery is for complications like dissection or aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin is diagnosed with GCA. He has no visual symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Prednisolone 40–60 mg daily with close monitoring of blood glucose
Prednisolone 15 mg daily to minimise steroid side effects
questions.explanation: Despite diabetes, high-dose oral prednisolone remains first-line for GCA; lower doses are inadequate, and tocilizumab is not first-line. Blood glucose should be monitored and managed concurrently but does not alter the steroid requirement.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with GCA on prednisolone 50 mg daily for 2 weeks develops symptoms of gastric irritation. Which drug is contraindicated as an addition to her regimen?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs such as ibuprofen increase the risk of GI bleeding when combined with corticosteroids and are therefore contraindicated. Proton pump inhibitors (lansoprazole) and bone protection (alendronic acid, calcium/vitamin D) are recommended; aspirin may be used for cardiovascular prophylaxis but is not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 3-week history of severe left-sided temporal headache, jaw claudication when eating, and scalp tenderness. She feels systemically unwell. Observations: HR 90, BP 135/80, temperature 37.5°C. CRP 120 mg/L, ESR 80 mm/h. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected GCA without visual loss, high-dose oral steroids should be started immediately to prevent ischaemic complications; biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks and is not required before treatment.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with a known history of hypertension presents with sudden loss of vision in his right eye over the past 2 hours. He also describes a dull headache and tenderness over his temples for the past month. On examination, visual acuity is hand movements only in the right eye, left eye 6/9. The right optic disc appears swollen. CRP 90 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/h. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start aspirin 300 mg and refer for retinal assessment
questions.explanation: In GCA with acute visual loss, high-dose IV steroids are first-line to salvage vision; oral steroids are insufficient in this emergency.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with known giant cell arteritis, diagnosed 3 months ago and currently on prednisolone 10 mg daily (tapering), presents with sudden severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back, with associated hypertension (BP 190/110 mmHg). Her pulse is 110 bpm. She looks distressed. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start IV labetalol for blood pressure control
questions.explanation: GCA predisposes to aortic aneurysm/dissection; chest/back pain with hypertension suggests dissection – imaging (CT aortogram) is the priority before steroid adjustment.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with giant cell arteritis diagnosed 6 months ago has been tapering prednisolone and is now on 5 mg daily. He presents with a 2-day history of recurrent headache and jaw claudication. CRP is 60 mg/L, ESR 50 mm/h. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Relapse of GCA requires prompt escalation of steroids to high dose (40-60 mg daily) to control inflammation; biopsy is unlikely to be helpful in confirmed disease.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with new-onset bilateral headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. She has no visual symptoms. ESR is 20 mm/h, CRP 5 mg/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Clinical suspicion for GCA outweighs normal inflammatory markers; high-dose steroids should be started promptly to prevent ischaemic complications.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of temporal headache, scalp tenderness, and pain on chewing. ESR is 65 mm/h. What single symptom is a red flag requiring immediate ophthalmology referral?
questions.explanation: Transient vision loss (amaurosis fugax) is a pre-ocular ischaemic symptom predicting imminent permanent visual loss if untreated; it mandates same-day ophthalmology assessment. Jaw claudication is characteristic but does not require immediate ophthalmology referral unless accompanied by visual symptoms.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with biopsy-proven GCA on high-dose prednisolone (40 mg daily) for 4 weeks presents with fever of 38.5°C, confusion, and worsening headache. What single physical examination finding is a red flag for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Neck stiffness raises concern for meningitis, a life-threatening infection in an immunocompromised patient. Temporal artery beading suggests active GCA but is not an acute emergency; papilloedema and cranial nerve palsies can occur in GCA but are less urgent than meningitis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known GCA complains of sudden severe interscapular back pain and feels faint. Her blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg in the right arm and 130/80 mmHg in the left arm. What physical sign is a red flag for the life-threatening complication of GCA?
questions.explanation: A difference of >20 mmHg in systolic BP between arms is a classic sign of aortic dissection, a catastrophic complication of GCA. Tender temporal artery indicates active arteritis but does not represent the immediate life threat.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with GCA on prednisolone 15 mg daily presents with acute, painless loss of vision in the right eye. On fundoscopy, which finding confirms anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION) as the cause?
questions.explanation: Optic disc oedema is characteristic of AION, the most common cause of permanent vision loss in GCA. A cherry red spot indicates central retinal artery occlusion, which is less common in GCA.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) for 6 months, controlled on 10 mg prednisolone, now reports new-onset bitemporal headache and pain when chewing. Which clinical sign is most specific for giant cell arteritis and warrants immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: A tender, nodular (beaded) temporal artery is a classic sign of GCA. In a PMR patient with new headache and jaw claudication, this finding strongly suggests GCA requiring urgent steroids to prevent vision loss. Reduced rotation and shoulder/hip stiffness are features of PMR, not GCA emergencies.
